text.title: sql-optimization Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for sql-optimization. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Measure before optimizing. Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to understand actual query performance, not guesses. Identify which plans are slow (full table scans, bitmap scans, sorts), not just which queries take long.
rule.rationale: Without measurement, optimization efforts target the wrong queries. EXPLAIN ANALYZE shows actual buffers used, rows scanned, and bottlenecks.

rule.statement: Optimize the right queries. Focus on frequent queries and those consuming most database resources (CPU, I/O), not just slow ones. A query that runs 10K times a day, taking 100ms, is more important than a nightly batch query taking 10 seconds.
rule.rationale: Database resources are finite. Optimize for total impact, not individual query time.

rule.statement: Indexes are not free. They speed reads but slow writes. Before adding an index, estimate write cost (slower inserts, updates, deletes) and read benefit. Don't add indexes speculatively.
rule.rationale: Over-indexing degrades write performance without guaranteed read benefit. Every index is a bet.

rule.statement: Reduce data movement. Filter early (WHERE clause on largest table first), join smart (avoid Cartesian products), fetch only needed columns (not SELECT *).
rule.rationale: Moving data between tables and across network is expensive. Keeping dataset small at each stage is usually more effective than optimizing individual operations.

rule.statement: Consider the full picture. Query optimization alone is incomplete. Connection pooling prevents query stalls from connection overhead. Caching prevents queries altogether. Materialized views pre-compute expensive aggregations. All are parts of the strategy.
rule.rationale: Micro-optimizing queries without macro-level strategy is inefficient. Sometimes adding cache is faster than optimizing a query.

rule.statement: Use EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT). ANALYZE runs the query and shows actual row counts. BUFFERS shows cache hits and disk I/O. TEXT format is readable; JSON is parseable by tools.
rule.rationale: ANALYZE is more accurate than EXPLAIN estimate alone. Estimates can be wildly wrong; actual execution data drives good decisions. BUFFERS reveals cache efficiency.

rule.statement: For WHERE clauses with multiple equality conditions, create a composite index matching the query: CREATE INDEX idx ON table(col1, col2) for queries on col1 AND col2. Order columns by selectivity: most selective first in equality conditions.
rule.rationale: Database optimizer can use the index for all conditions if they match the index order. If conditions are in wrong order, optimizer may use index for only the first, then scan for the rest.

rule.statement: For range queries (greater-than, less-than), put equality columns before range columns in the index: CREATE INDEX idx ON table(user_id, created_at) for WHERE user_id = ? AND created_at > ?
rule.rationale: Optimizer can use the equality part to narrow the range, then scan the range column. If range column comes first, the entire range is scanned then filtered by user_id.

rule.statement: Use covering indexes (INCLUDE clause) to avoid table lookups: CREATE INDEX idx ON table(col1, col2) INCLUDE (col3, col4) for queries selecting all four columns.
rule.rationale: Without INCLUDE, the index scan must look up the table to fetch non-indexed columns. Covering index makes index-only scan possible, eliminating a table access.

rule.statement: Use partial indexes for common filters: CREATE INDEX idx ON table(created_at) WHERE status = 'pending' for queries filtering on status = 'pending'.
rule.rationale: Partial index is smaller and faster to scan if only a fraction of rows match the filter. Saves space when most rows don't match the filter.

rule.statement: Use connection pooling with reasonable pool size (20-50 depending on workload). Set pool_pre_ping=True to verify connections before use. Set pool_recycle to recycle stale connections periodically.
rule.rationale: Creating a new database connection is expensive (TCP handshake, authentication, initialization). Reusing pooled connections is much faster. Pre-ping catches dropped connections early.

rule.statement: Use server-side cursors or result streaming for large result sets instead of loading all rows into memory.
rule.rationale: Large result sets that don't fit in memory can cause OOM. Streaming allows processing results in batches.

rule.statement: Monitor slow queries regularly using database logs or slow query log. Set a threshold (e.g., >100ms) and review queries exceeding it. Use monitoring tools to track query frequency and total time spent in each query.
rule.rationale: Queries change over time as data grows. A query fast on small data becomes slow on large data. Regular monitoring catches regressions early.

rule.statement: Update table statistics (ANALYZE in PostgreSQL, ANALYZE TABLE in MySQL) after bulk loads or significant schema changes. Stale statistics cause optimizer to make bad decisions.
rule.rationale: Optimizer relies on cardinality estimates. Stale stats can cause the optimizer to choose a bad plan, especially after bulk data changes.

rule.statement: Index maintenance: drop unused indexes (dead weight on writes), rebuild fragmented indexes if they slow down, review index columns for selectivity.
rule.rationale: Unused indexes slow writes without benefit. Fragmented indexes degrade performance. Periodic maintenance keeps the system healthy.

rule.statement: Test query changes on production-sized data before deployment. A query fast on test data may be slow on production data with different distribution.
rule.rationale: Test data is often too small to reveal performance issues. Always validate on realistic data sizes.
